question: [File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/controller/AccountController.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: account()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the @Min(value: 0) directive on EntityIdInput.num uses OnValidationErrorStrategy.RETURN_NULL, so supplying num=-1 silently returns null from account() rather than an error, causing a fee-calculation system that trusts the GraphQL response to treat the account as non-existent and waive fees?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/controller/AccountController.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: account()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the GraphQL schema exposes the balance field with a default unit of TINYBAR, but a consuming application may cache the response keyed only by account ID (not by unit), causing a GIGABAR-queried zero balance to be served as the cached response to a TINYBAR query, resulting in fee calculations using a zero balance?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/controller/AccountController.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: balance()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the HBAR conversion (tinybars / 100_000_000L) to receive a balance of 0 for any account with fewer than 100,000,000 tinybars (less than 1 HBAR), then use this zero-HBAR response to claim the account cannot pay HBAR-denominated fees, even though the account has sufficient tinybars?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/controller/AccountController.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: account()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the memo field returned by account() to extract human-readable metadata about fee arrangements or account purposes, using this information to identify accounts with special fee exemptions or custom fee schedules?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/controller/AccountController.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: account()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the type field (EntityType enum) returned by account() to confirm whether a queried entity is actually an ACCOUNT vs CONTRACT, and use CONTRACT accounts (which may have different fee structures) to bypass account-specific fee parameters?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/controller/AccountController.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: account()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the createdTimestamp field to determine the age of an account, using this to infer whether the account has accumulated staking rewards that affect its effective balance for fee purposes, and time fee-sensitive operations accordingly?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/controller/AccountController.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: account()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the pendingReward field (which also uses convertCurrency() via the Accountable interface) with unit=GIGABAR to receive a zero pending reward, then use this to argue that no reward-related fee adjustment should be applied to their account balance?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/controller/AccountController.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: account()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that account() returns Optional<Account> and maps through accountMapper::map, and that the AccountMapper uses MapStruct with AUTO_INHERIT_FROM_CONFIG, to trigger unexpected field mappings (e.g., ethereumNonce mapped to nonce) that expose fee-relevant fields not intended to be public?
